comment: 3-minute story film for the 2026 World Cup inaugural match: Mexico vs South Africa at the Estadio Azteca, June 11. Mystery-documentary tone for Brian (deep, resonant). The story: the Azteca's ghosts, the unfinished business of 2010, both squads, the two legendary mystery supporters (the Aztec Warrior and the Mandela Spirit), then the WorldCup26.world pitch and the pick-before-kickoff urgency. `at` = start time in seconds; mux.mjs time-fits each line to its slot.

lines.text: In Mexico City, there is a stadium older than most nations' World Cup dreams.
Pelé lifted a World Cup here. Maradona lifted another. No other ground on Earth can say that.
And on June the eleventh, the Estadio Azteca will open its third World Cup. No stadium has ever done that before.
Mexico against South Africa. The opening match. But this story did not begin here.
Sixteen years ago in Johannesburg, Tshabalala's thunderbolt lit up a World Cup opening night — and Mexico answered. One, one. Unfinished business.
Now the roles reverse — and Mexico brings legends to settle it.
Guillermo Ochoa. Forty years old. A record sixth World Cup — one last dance between the posts.
Chucky Lozano's lightning down the left. Giménez's cold finishing. Edson Álvarez, the machine in midfield.
One hundred thousand voices, at altitude, demanding history. That is what waits for the visitors.
But the Azteca keeps an older secret.
Before every great Mexican night, they say a figure appears in jaguar regalia. They call him the Aztec Warrior.
No ticket. No seat. No photographs — only blessings. And Mexico, they whisper, has never lost when he is seen.
Yet South Africa does not fly across the world to be a guest at someone else's party.
Ronwen Williams — the wall. A captain who saves penalties like he reads minds.
Percy Tau's magic. Lyle Foster's Premier League steel. Teboho Mokoena pulling every string.
Bafana Bafana arrive with nothing to lose — and those are the most dangerous teams a World Cup knows.
And they bring a guardian of their own.
Since nineteen ninety-four, at every Bafana match, a figure in a Madiba shirt stands quietly above the crowd.
They call him the Mandela Spirit. And when he raises his little flag — impossible things happen.
So, the warrior or the spirit? Four meetings between them. Two for Mexico. One draw. One for South Africa.
And here is what makes opening night interesting: Mexico pays nearly two times the points. South Africa — almost three.
This is where you stop watching, and start playing.
At WorldCup twenty-six dot world, you pick any three of the forty-eight nations — completely free.
Every win, every goal, every clean sheet scores for you — multiplied by your team's coefficient, and by how far they dare to go.
Back the favorites and climb steady. Or back an underdog like South Africa — and one upset can detonate your score.
But hear the clock. Every team locks at its first kickoff — and Mexico and South Africa lock first.
Pick before the first whistle at the Azteca, while all forty-eight nations are still on the table.
Free to join. Two minutes. No card. A live prize pool, and a leaderboard that runs all month.
The mystery kicks off June the eleventh. WorldCup twenty-six dot world. Pick your three.
